title: Differential equations: logistic model word problems
description: Solve word problems where a situation is modeled by a logistic differential equation.
topic_path: Differential equations: logistic model word problems

assessment_items.question_content:
The population $P(t)$ of fish in a pond satisfies the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=P\cdot\left(4-\dfrac{P}{6000} \right)$ where $t$ is measured in years and the initial population is $8000$ fish.

**What is the carrying capacity of the population?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] fish
hints:
The equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=P\cdot\left(4-\dfrac{P}{6000} \right)$ describes [[☃ definition 1]]. In logistic growth, the **carrying capacity** is the upper limit the quantity grows towards. Mathematically, this is $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}P(t)$.

![A logistic growth model is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to an inflection point, moves upward with decreasing steepness from the inflection point, then moves horizontally right. The horizontal portion of the curve represents carrying capacity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c007b4d290247deea62eb8306a577dde152d41f7)
For any logistic model $P$, the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ is equal to $0$. So at the carrying capacity, $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0$.

Let's solve $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0$ for $P$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dP}{dt}&=0
\\\\
P\cdot\left(4-\dfrac{P}{6000} \right)&=0
\\\\
P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad4-\dfrac{P}{6000}=0
\\\\
P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad P=24{,}000
\end{align}$

$P=0$ is the lower limit of the population, since there cannot be a negative number of fish. That leaves $24{,}000$ as our upper limit.
In conclusion, the carrying capacity of the fish population is $24{,}000$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The number $N(t)$ of trucks of a certain model that were sold within $t$ months satisfies the following logistic differential equation:

$\dfrac{dN}{dt}= N\cdot\left(90{,}000-\dfrac{3 N}{20{,}000} \right)$

There were initially $20{,}000{,}000$ trucks sold.

**What is the carrying capacity of trucks sold?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  trucks
hints:
The equation $\dfrac{dN}{dt}= N\cdot\left(90{,}000-\dfrac{3 N}{20{,}000} \right)$ describes [[☃ definition 1]]. In logistic growth, the **carrying capacity** is the upper limit the quantity grows towards. Mathematically, this is $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}N(t)$.

![A logistic growth model is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to an inflection point, moves upward with decreasing steepness from the inflection point, then moves horizontally right. The horizontal portion of the curve represents carrying capacity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c007b4d290247deea62eb8306a577dde152d41f7)
For any logistic model $N$, the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ is equal to $0$. So at the carrying capacity, $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=0$.

Let's solve $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=0$ for $N$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dN}{dt}&=0
\\\\
 N\cdot\left(90{,}000-\dfrac{3 N}{20{,}000} \right)&=0
\\\\
 N=0\quad&\text{or}\quad90{,}000-\dfrac{3 N}{20{,}000}=0
\\\\
N=0\quad&\text{or}\quad N=600{,}000{,}000
\end{align}$

$N=0$ is the lower limit of the number of trucks, since there cannot be a negative number of trucks. That leaves $600{,}000{,}000$ as our upper limit.
In conclusion, the carrying capacity of the number of trucks of a certain model is $600{,}000{,}000$ trucks.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jui-En encourages students to sign an anti-bullying pledge. The number $N(t)$ of students in the district to sign the pledge within $t$ weeks satisfies the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=\dfrac{1}{10} N\cdot\left(3-\dfrac{ N}{3400} \right)$ where the initial number of students is $20$.

**What is the carrying capacity of the population of students who have signed the pledge?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] students
hints:
The equation $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=\dfrac{1}{10} N\cdot\left(3-\dfrac{ N}{3400} \right)$ describes [[☃ definition 1]]. In logistic growth, the **carrying capacity** is the upper limit the quantity grows towards. Mathematically, this is $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}N(t)$.

![A logistic growth model is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to an inflection point, moves upward with decreasing steepness from the inflection point, then moves horizontally right. The horizontal portion of the curve represents carrying capacity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c007b4d290247deea62eb8306a577dde152d41f7)
For any logistic model $N$, the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ is equal to $0$. So at the carrying capacity, $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=0$.

Let's solve $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=0$ for $N$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dN}{dt}&=0
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{10} N\cdot\left(3-\dfrac{ N}{3400} \right)&=0
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{10} N=0\quad&\text{or}\quad3-\dfrac{ N}{3400}=0
\\\\
N=0\quad&\text{or}\quad N=10{,}200
\end{align}$

$N=0$ is the lower limit of the number of students, since there cannot be a negative number of students. That leaves $10{,}200$ as our upper limit.
In conclusion, the carrying capacity of the students in the district to sign the anti-bullying pledge is $10{,}200$ students.

assessment_items.question_content:
The number $P(t)$ of people who have heard about a certain contest after $t$ weeks satisfies the following logistic differential equation:

$\dfrac{dP}{dt}=\dfrac{1}{50} P\cdot\left(35{,}000-P \right)$ 

Initially, $1000$ people have heard about the contest.

**What is the carrying capacity of the population of people who have heard about the contest?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  people
hints:
The equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=\dfrac{1}{50} P\cdot\left(35{,}000-P \right)$ describes [[☃ definition 1]]. In logistic growth, the **carrying capacity** is the upper limit the quantity grows towards. Mathematically, this is $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}P(t)$.

![A logistic growth model is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to an inflection point, moves upward with decreasing steepness from the inflection point, then moves horizontally right. The horizontal portion of the curve represents carrying capacity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c007b4d290247deea62eb8306a577dde152d41f7)
For any logistic model $P$, the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ is equal to $0$. So at the carrying capacity, $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0$.

Let's solve $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0$ for $P$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dP}{dt}&=0
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{50} P\cdot\left(35{,}000-P \right)&=0
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{50} P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad35{,}000-P=0
\\\\
P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad P=35{,}000
\end{align}$

$P=0$ is the lower limit of the number of people, since there cannot be a negative number of people. That leaves $35{,}000$ as our upper limit.
In conclusion, the carrying capacity of the population of people who have heard about the contest is $35{,}000$ people.

assessment_items.question_content:
The population $P(t)$ of mice in a meadow after $t$ years satisfies the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=3P\cdot\left(1-\dfrac{P}{2500} \right)$ where the initial population is $1000$ mice.

**What is the population when it's growing the fastest?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] mice
hints:
Notice that $\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ as a function of $P$ is quadratic:

[[☃ image 1]]

So the maximum value of $\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ is obtained when the population is equal to the $P$-value of the vertex of the parabola.

We can find the $P$-value of the vertex by finding the roots and taking their average:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dP}{dt}&=0
\\\\
3P\cdot\left(1-\dfrac{P}{2500} \right)&=0
\\\\
3P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad1-\dfrac{P}{2500}=0
\\\\
P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad P=2500
\end{align}$

Now let's take the average:

$\dfrac{0+2500}{2}=1250$
In conclusion, the population when it's growing the fastest is $1250$ mice.

assessment_items.question_content:
The sales (in dollars) $S(c)$ of a certain brand of stove after the company shows $c$ commercials satisfies the following logistic differential equation:

$\dfrac{dS}{dc}=\dfrac{9}{1{,}000{,}000{,}000} S\cdot\left(20{,}000{,}000-S\right)$

where the initial sales are $\$250{,}000$.

**How much are the sales when they are growing the fastest?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars
hints:
Notice that $\dfrac{dS}{dc}$ as a function of $S$ is quadratic:

[[☃ image 1]]

So the maximum value of $\dfrac{dS}{dc}$ is obtained when the population is equal to the $S$-value of the vertex of the parabola.

We can find the $S$-value of the vertex by finding the roots and taking their average:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dS}{dc}&=0
\\\\
\dfrac{9}{1{,}000{,}000{,}000} S\cdot\left(20{,}000{,}000-S \right)&=0
\\\\
\dfrac{9}{1{,}000{,}000{,}000} S=0\quad&\text{or}\quad 20{,}000{,}000-S=0
\\\\
S=0\quad&\text{or}\quad S=20{,}000{,}000
\end{align}$

Now let's take the average:

$\dfrac{0+20{,}000{,}000}{2}=10{,}000{,}000$
In conclusion, the sales when they are growing the fastest are $10{,}000{,}000$ dollars.

assessment_items.question_content:
The number $N(t)$ of people who have adopted a certain fashion style after $t$ months satisfies the logistic differential equation:

 $\dfrac{dN}{dt}= N\cdot\left(0.1-\dfrac{ N}{1{,}800{,}000} \right)$ 

Initially, there were $3000$  people who had adopted the style.

**What is the number of people who have adopted the style when it's growing the fastest?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  people
hints:
Notice that $\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ as a function of $N$ is quadratic:

[[☃ image 1]]

So the maximum value of $\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ is obtained when the number of people is equal to the $N$-value of the vertex of the parabola.

We can find the $N$-value of the vertex by finding the roots and taking their average:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dN}{dt}&=0
\\\\
 N\cdot\left(0.1-\dfrac{ N}{1{,}800{,}000} \right)&=0
\\\\
 N=0\quad&\text{or}\quad0.1-\dfrac{ N}{1{,}800{,}000}=0
\\\\
N=0\quad&\text{or}\quad N=180{,}000
\end{align}$

Now let's take the average:

$\dfrac{0+180{,}000}{2}=90{,}000$
In conclusion, the number of people when it's growing the fastest is $90{,}000$ people.

assessment_items.question_content:
The population $P(t)$ of worms in a compost pile after $t$ years satisfies the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}= P\cdot\left(2-\dfrac{ P}{2250} \right)$ where the initial population is $18$ worms.

**What is the population when it's growing the fastest?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] worms
hints:
Notice that $\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ as a function of $P$ is quadratic:

[[☃ image 1]]

So the maximum value of $\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ is obtained when the population is equal to the $P$-value of the vertex of the parabola.

We can find the $P$-value of the vertex by finding the roots and taking their average:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dP}{dt}&=0
\\\\
 P\cdot\left(2-\dfrac{ P}{2250} \right)&=0
\\\\
 P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad2-\dfrac{ P}{2250}=0
\\\\
P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad P=4500
\end{align}$

Now let's take the average:

$\dfrac{0+4500}{2}=2250$
In conclusion, the population when it's growing the fastest is $2250$ worms.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph could be a solution to the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=P\cdot\left( 2-\dfrac{P}{10} \right)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=P\cdot\left( 2-\dfrac{P}{10} \right)$ describes a **logistic growth** function. This is a function that is first concave up (i.e. its rate increases) but then it becomes concave down so it approaches (but never really reaches) a certain limit called the **carrying capacity**. The inflection point occurs when our dependent variable $P$ is half of the carrying capacity.

![A logistic growth model is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to an inflection point, moves upward with decreasing steepness from the inflection point, then moves horizontally right. The horizontal portion of the curve represents carrying capacity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/91ce84b1c9162e8e3da06662824c836349acb0d8)

**Note:** We may see only a portion of the graph at a time, so let's consider the carrying capacity and the value of $P$ at the inflection point to select the best graph.

To find the carrying capacity, we will use the fact that for any logistic model $P$, the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ is equal to $0$. So at the carrying capacity, $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0$.

Let's solve $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dP}{dt}&=0
\\\\
P\cdot\left( 2-\dfrac{P}{10} \right)&=0
\\
\\
P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad2-\dfrac{P}{10}=0
\\\\
P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad P=20
\end{align}$

$P=0$ is the lower limit of a possible solution, but an upper limit must be $20$.

Since the carrying capacity is $P=20$, the inflection point should occur when $P=\dfrac{20}{2}=10$. We can eliminate both of these options because they are still concave up when $P$ is greater than $10$.

![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 20.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/25398d9635c62dfcf5f7656424fe2dd6ca0d4109) ![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 15.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ace61c789a6b63ee883574e5239cce3edcb43a92)

The remaining two options differ on their upper limit. Which has the correct carrying capacity?

In conclusion, this graph could be a solution to the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=P\cdot\left( 2-\dfrac{P}{10} \right)$:

![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 15, moves upward with decreasing steepness to a point corresponding to y = 20, then moves horizontally right.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e1cec063c6da14075288beabf37dde5d6f7aae6)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph could be a solution to the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=5 N\cdot\left(1-\dfrac{N}{8} \right)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The equation $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=5 N\cdot\left(1-\dfrac{N}{8} \right)$ describes a **logistic growth** function. This is a function that is first concave up (i.e. its rate increases) but then it becomes concave down so it approaches (but never really reaches) a certain limit called the **carrying capacity**. The inflection point occurs when our dependent variable $N$ is half of the carrying capacity.

![A logistic growth model is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to an inflection point, moves upward with decreasing steepness from the inflection point, then moves horizontally right. The horizontal portion of the curve represents carrying capacity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/91ce84b1c9162e8e3da06662824c836349acb0d8)

**Note:** We may see only a portion of the graph at a time, so let's consider the carrying capacity and the value of $N$ at the inflection point to select the best graph.

To find the carrying capacity, we will use the fact that for any logistic model $N$, the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ is equal to $0$. So at the carrying capacity, $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=0$.

Let's solve $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=0$ for $N$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dN}{dt}&=0
\\\\
5 N\cdot\left(1-\dfrac{N}{8} \right)&=0
\\\\
5 N=0\quad&\text{or}\quad1-\dfrac{N}{8}=0
\\\\
N=0\quad&\text{or}\quad N=8
\end{align}$

$N=0$ is the lower limit of a possible solution, but an upper limit must be $8$.

Since the carrying capacity is $N=8$, the inflection point should occur when $N=\dfrac{8}{2}=4$. We can eliminate both of these options because they are still concave up when $N$ is greater than $4$.

![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bbcd05bfacb88a8a948f1ea73d5c2fbdd96a99b5) ![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 8.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ac1b6c58efbab8912c92e7c715c6ddf60a83bcf8)

The remaining two options differ on their upper limit. Which has the correct carrying capacity?

In conclusion, this graph could be a solution to the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dN}{dt}=5 N\cdot\left(1-\dfrac{N}{8} \right)$:

![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 6, moves upward with decreasing steepness to a point corresponding to y = 8, then moves horizontally right.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aea6144ebea32229ef0f468d4cb662e34051eaf5)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph could be a solution to the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dy}{dt}= y\cdot\left(10-\dfrac{y}{60} \right)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **logistic growth** function is first concave up (i.e. its rate increases) but then it becomes concave down so it approaches (but never really reaches) a certain limit called the carrying capacity. 

![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to an inflection point, moves upward with decreasing steepness from the inflection point, then moves horizontally right. The horizontal portion of the curve represents carrying capacity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/91ce84b1c9162e8e3da06662824c836349acb0d8)

So we can immediately eliminate these two options:

![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a line. The line moves diagonally upward from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 400.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/32510291c891637f87828d272ca553632e2f5836) ![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a line. The line moves diagonally upward from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 600.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5913fd25bd8a770e7c963a9148d821fba09adbc5)

The remaining two options differ on their upper limit. Let's analyze the equation to see what that upper limit should be.

To find the carrying capacity, we will use the fact that for any logistic model $y$, the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}\dfrac{dy}{dt}$ is equal to $0$. So at the carrying capacity, $\dfrac{dy}{dt}=0$.

Let's solve $\dfrac{dy}{dt}=0$ for $y$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=0
\\\\
 y\cdot\left(10-\dfrac{y}{60} \right)&=0
\\\\
 y=0\quad&\text{or}\quad10-\dfrac{y}{60}=0
\\\\
y=0\quad&\text{or}\quad y=600
\end{align}$

$y=0$ is the lower limit of a possible solution, but an upper limit must be $600$.

In conclusion, this graph could be a solution to the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dy}{dt}= y\cdot\left(10-\dfrac{y}{60} \right)$:

![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 300, moves upward with decreasing steepness to a point corresponding to y = 600, then moves horizontally right.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e415160862cde2c3dcf812151683098dde78fbcf)

The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 300, moves upward with decreasing steepness to a point corresponding to y = 600, then moves horizontally right.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e415160862cde2c3dcf812151683098dde78fbcf)

The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 200, moves upward with decreasing steepness to a point corresponding to y = 400, then moves horizontally right.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c20ebdcc2a08287a18afb46560f9bae581d20dda)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which graph could be a solution to the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=2 P\cdot\left(50-P \right)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A **logistic growth** function is first concave up (i.e. its rate increases) but then it becomes concave down so it approaches (but never really reaches) a certain limit called the carrying capacity. 

![A logistic growth model is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to an inflection point, moves upward with decreasing steepness from the inflection point, then moves horizontally right. The horizontal portion of the curve represents carrying capacity.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/91ce84b1c9162e8e3da06662824c836349acb0d8)

So we can immediately eliminate these two options:

![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with decreasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to approximately y = 25, then moves upward with increasing steepness to a point corresponding to y = 50.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/25b000846f6b2a713494dbe27a50ea5c10b59054) ![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with decreasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to approximately y = 30, then moves upward with increasing steepness to a point corresponding to y = 60.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/71559ba592d2139dc1b6f42a8ac27718ccf65132)

The remaining two options differ on their upper limit. Let's analyze the equation to see what that upper limit should be.

To find the carrying capacity, we will use the fact that for any logistic model $P$, the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ is equal to $0$. So at the carrying capacity, $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0$.

Let's solve $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0$ for $P$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dP}{dt}&=0
\\\\
2 P\cdot\left(50-P \right)&=0
\\\\
2 P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad50-P=0
\\\\
P=0\quad&\text{or}\quad P=50
\end{align}$

$P=0$ is the lower limit of a possible solution, but an upper limit must be $50$.

In conclusion, this graph could be a solution to the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=2 P\cdot\left(50-P\right)$:

![A function is graphed. The horizontal axis is represented by t. The graph is a curve. The curve moves upward with increasing steepness from the origin to a point corresponding to y = 25, moves upward with decreasing steepness to a point corresponding to y = 50, then moves horizontally right.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a539135c0511d1ac264f0ab564d7d04d4aa62b23)
